cataloging_subject.label: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The repository item is Max Förster's article-level edited selection on printed pp. 50–61, not one medieval poem. It combines German editorial argument and apparatus with a reconstructed five-stanza B-version core and introductory couplet, two blocks of shared additional B-version stanzas, a complete seven-stanza Corpus Christi College MS 237 text mediated by Arthur Napier's transcript, an independent nine-stanza poem from St John's College MS E.24 collated against the Public Record Office roll, and only the first stanza of Murray's A version. Abstract works and versions, physical witnesses, intermediary transcripts, Förster's 1919 edition, the Michigan encoding, and the repository artifact remain distinct layers.

catalog_summary.title: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous historical poets
catalog_summary.date: Distinct medieval works and version units; no single composition date established; edited selection published 1919
catalog_summary.region: Unknown; witness origins and dialects are not transferred to the abstract works

form_statements.description: German prose commentary and apparatus surrounding Middle English verse texts

subject_statements.label: Transience of the world and worldly vanity

scope.description: Förster's reconstruction, grouping of stanza layers, witness apparatus, and cross-version comparison

summaries.value: Förster's edited selection reconstructs an early five-stanza B-version core of 'Erthe upon Erthe', groups two successive bodies of shared added stanzas, prints a complete seven-stanza Oxford Corpus Christi College text, edits a formally independent nine-stanza 'Whan erþe' poem from St John's College MS E.24 with variants from a Public Record Office roll, and samples the A version. The verse repeatedly figures the human body as earth in order to oppose worldly rank and possession to death, burial, judgment, repentance, and salvation.

Seven modeled parts: a noncontiguous German editorial frame; a reconstructed B-version core of five quatrains plus a two-line English introductory epigraph; two shared added-stanza blocks of two and five quatrains; a seven-quatrain Corpus Christi College MS 237 text; a nine-quatrain independent poem plus one four-line Public Record Office variant stanza nested in its apparatus; and one six-line sample stanza of Murray's A version.

The catalog record describes Förster's edited selection as a whole while preserving six historical verse units as parts. The 1919 publication date and German editorial language characterize the edition layer; manuscript dates, loci, origins, and dialects characterize witnesses. None supplies an unqualified composition date, place, or author for all selected medieval material.

extent.description: The XML has three DIV1 sections, two DIV2, three DIV3, 12 PB, 146 NOTE, 1,192 HI, two MILESTONE, 29 direct main-text LG, and 120 direct main-text L. A four-line Public Record Office-only stanza is nested as one LG inside a NOTE; counting that structurally encoded apparatus stanza gives 30 LG and 124 L. The two-line English epigraph before the reconstructed B-version core contributes two L but no LG. Six historical verse units are counted as numbered items; the discontinuous editorial frame has item_count 0 but contributes one prose-only component.

content_parts.label: Förster's editorial frame and critical apparatus
content_parts.summary: Noncontiguous German headings, headnotes, witness descriptions, textual argument, line and stanza commentary, and variant notes surrounding all selected texts.

locators.value: BODY headings, HEADNOTE, NOTE, DATELINE, and prose P elements across DIV1[1]–DIV1[3]

notes: item_count 0 records that this frame is not a separately numbered historical text while retaining it in prose/verse extent totals.

content_parts.label: Five-stanza B-version Urform reconstruction
content_parts.summary: Förster's reconstructed early B-version core: a two-line English introductory epigraph followed by five monorhyming quatrains, with a Latin heading and extensive multi-witness apparatus.
notes: The heading 'Urform' expresses Förster's reconstruction, not a surviving authorial archetype or a single diplomatic transcription.

content_parts.label: Shared added stanzas of the second manuscript group
content_parts.summary: Two four-line stanzas that Förster treats as a shared secondary accretion in a manuscript group of the B-version tradition.

content_parts.label: Shared added stanzas of the third manuscript group
content_parts.summary: Five four-line stanzas that Förster treats as a further shared accretion in a third manuscript group of the B-version tradition.

content_parts.label: Seven-stanza Oxford Corpus Christi College MS 237 text
content_parts.summary: A complete seven-quatrain B-version witness text printed by Förster from Arthur Napier's transcript rather than from direct inspection of the codex.

locators.value: current f. 240v; Förster's old foliation f. 233v

notes: Napier's transcript is modeled as an intermediary in the lineage manifest.

content_parts.label: Independent nine-stanza poem beginning 'Whan erþe hauet erþe wiþ woo by-ȝute'
content_parts.summary: Förster's edition of a formally independent nine-quatrain poem from St John's College MS E.24, collated against the Public Record Office roll; the apparatus also embeds one four-line stanza found only in the roll.
notes: Direct main text comprises nine LG and 36 L; the structural metrics include the one LG/four L Public Record Office-only apparatus stanza nested inside the fourth stanza's note.

content_parts.label: First-stanza sample of Murray's A version
content_parts.summary: Only the first six-line aaaabb stanza of the seven-stanza A version, printed by Förster through Hilda M. R. Murray's 1911 edition.

locators.value: ff. 62r–63v; Förster prints only the first stanza

related_works.title: Erthe upon Erthe, B version
notes: The selection contains a reconstructed five-stanza core, shared added blocks, and one complete seven-stanza witness text rather than DIMEV's full sixteen-quatrain abstract B version.

related_works.title: Erthe upon Erthe, B version with introductory couplet
notes: DIMEV treats the St John's material at ff. 44r–45r as one fourteen-quatrain B-version witness with a couplet; Förster separates the first five stanzas from the following nine-stanza independent poem.

related_works.title: Erthe upon Erthe, nine monorhyming quatrains
Erthe upon Erthe, A version
notes: Only the first stanza is present in CME00138.

related_works.title: Erthe upon Erthe, C version
notes: Förster's B-version apparatus cites readings from witnesses that DIMEV now classifies in the C-version record; the selection does not print an abstract C-version text.

One LG/four L stanza is nested in apparatus, giving structural totals of 30 LG and 124 L; the opening English couplet contributes two L but no LG.

evidence.citation: Max Förster, ‘Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes “Earth upon Earth”,’ Archiv 138 (1919): 39–61, especially 50–61.
evidence.summary: Direct article scan evidence for title, editor, publication, selected boundary, five-stanza reconstruction, manuscript grouping, intermediary transcripts, independent-poem judgment, complete St John's text and apparatus, and A-version sample.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 1170, 1171, 6292, 6293, and 6369.
evidence.summary: Versioned scholarly evidence for incipits, A/B/C and independent-work classifications, verse forms, subjects, witness loci, current foliation, and the St John's work-boundary conflict.

evidence.citation: Hilda M. R. Murray, The Middle English Poem Erthe upon Erthe, EETS OS 141 (1911).
evidence.summary: Supports the A-version source and the textual mediation of witnesses and transcripts that Förster inherited from Murray.

evidence.citation: St John's College Cambridge MS E.24 access and work-boundary evidence.
evidence.summary: Supports the present holding and public enquiry route; Förster and DIMEV supply the textual locus and divergent modeling of ff. 44r–45r.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, E 175/11/16, record C3315697.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the surviving roll, current shelfmark, physical description, access route, and the need to keep the 1311–12 Ordinances from being used as an unqualified date for the later poem on membrane 4d.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue and complete IIIF facsimile, Harley MS 913.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the A-version witness's current date, support, exact ff. 62r–63v locus, and complete public facsimile coverage.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English item CME00138.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the digital item, stable identifier, and public access layer.

open_questions.question: Should St John's College MS E.24, ff. 44r–45r, be modeled as Förster's five-stanza B-version core followed by an independent nine-stanza poem, or as DIMEV 6369's single fourteen-quatrain B-version witness with an introductory couplet?
next_steps: Obtain and inspect complete images of ff. 44r–45r, including layout, headings, spacing, and scribal transitions.
Compare the formal, lexical, and transmission evidence used by Förster with DIMEV's witness-level classification.
Retain both explicit models and avoid silently merging the two conceptual work units.

open_questions.question: Can a composition date or place be established for Förster's reconstructed five-stanza B-version core independently of the dates, origins, and dialects of its surviving witnesses?
next_steps: Reassess Förster's tentative thirteenth-century and North or North-Midlands hypothesis against later textual and linguistic scholarship.
Separate archetype reconstruction from the date and localization of each witness reading.
Add composition facets only when work-level evidence supports them.

open_questions.question: Do the two shared added-stanza blocks and the individual witness additions represent one reviser, several anonymous poets, or independent accretions?
next_steps: Collate each stanza's full witness distribution against Förster's sigla and current DIMEV witness assignments.
Compare meter, rhyme, diction, and devotional emphasis across the reconstructed core and added layers.
Do not assign the collective anonymous B-version responsibility to a single person without stronger evidence.

notes: The cataloging subject is the article-level edited selection encoded on Förster's pp. 50–61, not the complete 1919 article on pp. 39–61 and not one medieval authored poem.
The 1919 publication date and German editorial language are scoped to Förster's edition layer and excluded from unqualified medieval-work discovery facets.
Witness dates, origins, dialects, present holdings, and foliation are not composition facts for the abstract works or versions.
Structural verse totals include the four-line Public Record Office-only stanza nested inside a NOTE; direct main-text totals remain 29 LG and 120 L.
Förster's tentative thirteenth-century, northern or North-Midlands Urform hypothesis remains an open research question rather than a date or place statement.
The main Ordinances on TNA E 175/11/16 date to 1311–12, but the Earth-upon-Earth material is a later addition on membrane 4d; the administrative document date is not transferred to the poem.
